data.title: SPXX: Much Better Choices Out There
data.description:
Thesis

We have not covered the Nuveen S&P 500 Dynamic Overwrite Fund (NYSE:SPXX) for two years now. With the buy-write funds market now overrun by options, we are going to revisit this closed end fund. In today's article we are going to review the CEF's current composition, its performance and analytics, and articulate why an investor has a plethora of other better choices.

Some analytics first

  • AUM: $0.33
  • Expense ratio: 0.97% (please note that buy-write funds do not have any leverage, thus this is the true expense ratio for the CEF)
  • St Dev: 12%
  • Sharpe: 0.58
  • Discount: -2.5%
  • z-stat: 0.8

The CEF is trading at a slight discount to NAV, but towards the top of the range. Please note the drag from the expense ratio.

Current CEF composition

Let us first go back to the stated goal for the fund:

The Fund is designed to offer regular distributions through a strategy that seeks attractive total return with less volatility than the S&P 500 Index by investing in a U.S. equity portfolio that seeks to substantially replicate the price movements of the S&P 500 Index, as well as selling call options on 35%-75% of the notional value of the Fund's equity portfolio (with a 55% long-term target) in an effort to enhance the Fund's risk-adjusted returns.

The fund's current composition reminds us of the S&P 500 sectoral parsing:

Information technology is the largest sector at 32%, followed by Financials at 17% and Communication Services at 11%. More importantly though, the name only writes options on a part of the portfolio:

Only 59% of the portfolio is currently covered by calls, with the rest being a pure long position. Please note the fund writes very high delta options, with a call strike of 99% versus spot. This simply means it leaves no upside if the market remains constant or goes up. Other buy-write names write 102% strikes in order to manage some upside for the name. The CEF writes on average 2-week options via the 15 days to expiration time period.

Premium/Discount to NAV performance

The CEF is trading almost flat to NAV:

Please note that today's levels are close to the top of their recent range. The CEF only trades at a premium when the market sells-off. Why? Because of its conservative set-up for the option strikes. We can see that in 2022 when the CEF was at a premium, and again in 2018 when we had another market sell-off.

Performance - guess who is dead last?

A 'perfect' buy-write fund is supposed to produce exactly the same return as the S&P 500, but instead of seeing capital gains, an investor would see a monthly flow of dividends. There is no such thing as a 'perfect' buy-write fund, but we can look at relative performance:

When looking at the CEF we assembled a cohort of peers that includes the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI), the Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income Fund (EOI) and the Eaton Vance Tax managed Equity Fund (ETY). If we look at the above graph we will notice who comes in dead last when total return is used - it is SPXX. The name has a total return this year of 4.8%, versus the 10.7% posted by ETY, the best performer.

SPXX did have a slightly shallower drawdown this year, but all the cohort exhibited fairly similar moves:

Please note that EOI and ETY had the largest drawdowns from the cohort from an absolute number figure, while SPXX experienced a -17% draw. Buy-write funds have a very similar downside to the index because they disburse option premiums as dividends, and the buffer provided by the current overlay is small. Unless a fund does very long dated options with a high delta (we are not aware of any), expect buy-write names to have a similar downside to the index outright.

Macro picture is not helpful

Since SPXX is a buy-write fund that bases its performance on the S&P 500, let us have a look at the current valuation metrics for the index:

As per Bloomberg the index is currently trading 35% above its long-term average, and as we can see from the above chart its P/E ratio is close to historic highs again. While this situation can persist for some time, please note there is always mean reversion in the market. As simple as it might sound, the old adage 'Buy low, sell high' applies to the index as well when it comes to P/E ratios. Can the market continue to trade stretched? Absolutely. Will there be a mean reversion in the next 12 months? We strongly believe so.

Although SPXX trades a bit better during sell-offs due to its conservative delta on the options, it will not be able to escape the market downturn, as seen in April 2025.

Conclusion

SPXX is an equities buy-write fund. The name aims to extract dividends from the S&P 500 via writing covered calls. The fund currently writes options on only 59% of its portfolio, but does so with very high deltas and 99% of spot strike options. This set-up has seen the CEF lag in the past year, with the market rally better captured by the fund's peers. We do not see much value in paying 0.97% in expenses for this fund when there are much better options presented in the article. The overall macro is also not helpful for SPXX given stretched overall valuations. We are a 'Sell' for SPXX at the current juncture.

data.title: American Financial Group: Another Mixed Quarter (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of American Financial Group (NYSE:AFG) have been a modest performer this year, trading essentially flat, though it has paid special dividends. Shares are down over 15% from their highs in late 2024, and increasing worries about P&C premium inflation have weighed on insurance stocks lately. I last covered shares of AFG in May, reiterating the stock as a “buy,” but performance has been disappointing with a 2% total return significantly underperforming the market’s 11% rally. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit AFG. I am less bullish.

In the company’s second quarter, American Financial Group earned $2.07, which beat estimates by a penny as revenue grew by 1% to $1.92 billion. There was $0.07 of noncore items for $2.14 in core earnings, down $0.42 from last year. Frankly, this was a mixed quarter in keeping with a series of less-than-perfect results that we have seen in recent months. Alternative investments earned just $8 million from $33 million a year ago, a $0.24 headwind to earnings. AFG also had $11 million of prior year favorable developments down from $35 million last year. Apart from these items, results were similar to a year ago.

Underwriting was mixed

Overall, AFG generated a core return on equity of 15.5% as net written premiums grew by 7% to $1.8 billion. AFG’s average renewal rate was up 7%, so premium growth was entirely driven by price increases with its real exposure essentially unchanged over the past year. In total, AFG generated a $114 million underwriting profit, down by $37 million from last year.

Despite a favorable pricing environment, AFG’s total combined ratio of 93.1% was up from 90.5% last year. There were 2.3% of catastrophe losses, similar to last year, and there were 0.7% of favorable prior year developments, 1.6% less than last year. Overall, we saw modest margin degradation. I like to see AFG operate in the 90-92.5% area, so this quarter was a bit weaker than where AFG should seek to operate.

Looking at segment results, Property & Transportation net written premiums grew by 10% to $759 million. The combined ratio here rose by 250 bps to 95.2%. There was a much smaller prior-year favorable reserve adjustment, which added 410 bps to the combined ratio. The underwriting profit shrunk to $27 million from $40 million last year. Admittedly, crop results were very strong last year, creating a difficult comparison. AFG generated an 8% renewal rate increase in transportation, which has been a segment pressured by ongoing social inflation.

Specialty casualty premiums rose by a very modest 2% to $765 million. The calendar year combined ratio increased to 93.9% from 89.1%. There was also a 140bps headwind due to a build in reserve of $10 million due to unfavorable prior year developments. There were $49 million in profits, down from $86 million last year. Excess and surplus profitability was lower; this is an area that has seen more competitive pricing and has likely passed peak profits.

Specialty Financial premiums were the standout with premiums up 12% to $279 million and the combined ratio falling by 360 bps to 86.1%. This was almost entirely driven by $9 million of favorable prior year developments. Interestingly in this segment, pricing was flat, meaning that AFG actively added exposure, given how profitable policies are. I am cautious on the magnitude of growth here, given the lack of pricing increases, and I expect we are near peak profitability.

Rate increases have likely peaked

Insurance premium inflation tends to lag inflation in the real economy because premiums are set on a 6-12 month basis typically. The initial post-COVID inflation spike initially compressed insurers’ margins, but they aggressively recaptured it during 2023-24 with premium inflation passing 6%. It has more recently been decelerating and is now just a bit above 3%.

This deceleration has increased worries that the insurance sector may be seeing its margins peak. Now, I think most of the underwriting margin expansion has occurred already after years of strong premium inflation. However, I would emphasize that premium inflation of 3+% is still sufficient to match overall inflation. As such, I do not see margin compression on the horizon. Instead, I see combined ratios remaining near current levels, which are quite healthy for the industry, though this could make it more difficult for AFG to get back to the low end of my 90-92.5% combined ratio target.

Investment performance is mixed

Excluding alternatives, net investment income was up 10%. AFG has a $16 billion portfolio of cash and investments, which has benefited from reinvesting maturities in the current elevated interest rate environment. It has $11 billion of fixed maturity securities at a 3 year duration, and the vast majority of the yield gains have now occurred. About 98% of the portfolio is investment grade, and I see limited risk of losses here.

Rather, the disappointment sits outside of AFG’s fixed income portfolio. In Q2, its annualized return of alternatives was just 1.2%. Its ownership of multifamily apartment buildings has been challenged by increased supply. As you can see below, it owns $1.2 billion of multifamily rental units, and occupancy is down to 92%, similar to the national average. Because of new supply, rent growth has disappointed my expectations this year, and I expect less than 2% rental growth over the next year. As such, I expected muted alternative returns to continue.

Additionally, in June, AFG announced that it was terminating the sale of the Charleston Harbor Resort & Marina, a property held within its investment portfolio. This sale was one of the bigger pieces of good news in its Q1 release. It was supposed to close in Q3 and generate a $1.20 per share gain, or about $100 million. While the company continues to explore strategic alternatives for the site, I no longer expect a sale to occur this year. I had anticipated most of that $1.20 to be paid out in special dividends, and that will no longer occur.

AFG is a hold at current levels

AFG pays an $0.80 base dividend for a 2.5% dividend yield. It also did $39 million of buybacks during the quarter, and its share count is down about 0.5% from last year. Management reiterated its willingness to pay special dividends, and I see scope for a payment of up to $2 in the second half of the year, given excess capital and core earnings. Its balance sheet remains strong with just $1.5 billion of debt.

Back in May, I expected AFG to earn $10-$10.50 this year, excluding the marina sale, which would support $7.50 of capital returns. I now see AFG earning closer to $9.50 given weaker underwriting results outside of financial segments as well as weak pricing within financial products. Plus, the rental outlook is more tepid, which may reduce investment income.

At over 13x earnings, I do not see AFG as particularly compelling. With a similar valuation and better underwriting track record, I would rather just own Chubb (CB). I do not see these results sparking a reacceleration in shares after recent disappointing performance, especially given lessening premium inflation. As such, I am downgrading shares to a “hold.” With capital returns, there is no rush to sell, but I would not put new money to work into AFG until we see cleaner underwriting results on a more consistent basis.

data.title: Corcept's Q2 Demand Grows 60%, Penetrating Large Type 2 Diabetes Market Early
data.description:
Corcept Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CORT) ($67.17/share) announced on 7/31/25 what appeared to be mixed results for Q2 2025 ending 6/30/25 as its third-party specialty pharmacy could not meet the surge in demand from the rapid growth in prescriptions and average selling prices were 20% lower vs. Q2 2024.

On the Q2 2025 Conference call, Corcept management said prescription volume (# tablets sold) grew by +49% in Q2 2025 vs. Q2 2024.

Management stated that the outside 3rd party specialty pharmacy was again unable to meet rapidly growing demand and couldn't deliver $15 million of additional revenue as the rate of prescription growth accelerated in Q2 vs. Q1 when the pharmacy also could not meet demand.

Management stated Corcept's outside pharmacy will also likely not be able to meet demand in Q3 and the company may only catch up with its accelerating growth in demand in Q4 as the original pharmacy expands further and Corcept adds a 2nd pharmacy in the 4th Quarter.

Corcept disclosed that average selling prices were down 20% on a weighted basis in Q2 vs. last year due to 30% discounts for the 2/3 of volume now under its own Authorized Generic program.

With unfulfilled demand and lower selling prices, management reduced full year 2025 revenue guidance by $50 million from $900-950 million to $850-900 million. (The midpoint of $875 million would still reflect annual revenue growth of 29.6% vs. $675 million of revenues in 2024.)

However, we are actually encouraged by today's report because going into the Q2 print we were not sure how many months or even years it would take for doctors who can be very conservative about changing medical practices to accept the recently published studies showing that hypercortisolism is seriously underdiagnosed and 60 times more prevalent than previously suspected.

From today's announced accelerating volume growth rate, it appears that the medical community is responding earlier than we expected to reports showing that 25% of their "difficult to treat" Type 2 diabetic patients are likely suffering from excess cortisol production and should be tested to see if they could benefit from Corcept's medication. (See our prior Corcept Seeking Alpha articles "Corcept Therapeutics - Three Dynamic Catalysts to Reaccelarate Growth", "Corcept: Key Announcements Coming Soon" and "It's Still Early For Corcept: With Multiple Growth Opportunities Ahead, It's No Longer Just a One Drug Wonder")

We won't know until we see another couple of quarters and the pharmacy capacity catches up with script demand, but we suspect that the potential surge in demand from our estimate of 1.2 million underdiagnosed Type 2 diabetic patients with hypercortisolism (see our Seeking Alpha initiation report September 15, 2024) may have already begun.

We reiterate the potential scale effect for the new 1.2 million person TAM (total available market) is huge relative to the tiny base of only about 2000 patients we estimated that Corcept was treating a couple years ago. Any material penetration of this new Type 2 diabetic market could provide many years of very rapid double digit upside to revenue and earnings growth.

What was reported

Revenues for Q2 ending June 30, 2025 were $194.4 million, up 18.7% vs Q2 2024 and slightly below the Wall Street estimate of $199.4 million and our estimate of $196.2 million.

Q2 2025 EPS was $0.29/share above the Wall Street estimate of $0.19/share and our estimate of $0.21/share on lower taxes..

Volume of tablets dispensed increased 49% in Q2 2025 vs Q2 2024

From the Conference call

Average selling price down 20% vs year ago as 2/3 of sales were at Corcept's "Authorized Generic" price 30% lower than the posted price and 1/3 of sales at the regular posted price.

The 3rd party specialty pharmacy was unable to fulfill $15 million of sales in Q2.

We were also encouraged to hear that Corcept management has already increased its salesforce from 60 at the beginning of 2024 to 145 clinical specialists currently and they "plan to have 175 by yearend". This tripling of sales capacity helps explain why growth in prescriptions appears to be accelerating.

Pro Forma Q2 Revenue Growth Closer to +52.9%

Reported revenues of $194.4 million grew +18.7% in Q2 2025 vs. $163.8 million in Q2 2024.

Q2 Revenue growth would have been $15 million higher or $209.4 million, up 9.1% higher for a growth rate of +27.8% if the pharmacy would have been able to deliver the $15 million of unfulfilled orders.

Q2 revenue growth would have been an additional 20% higher on both the fulfilled and the unfulfilled scripts if Corcept did not reduce blended average selling prices by 20% in Q2 vs. a year ago by offering an Authorized Generic, meaning that revenues would have grown by +52.9% to $250.9 million vs. Q2 2024 IF Corcept had not discounted prices and the pharmacy had met demand.*

Pro Forma Physical Volume Growth Closer to +60%

Reported 49% growth in volume in Q2 2025 vs, Q2 2024.

Physical volume growth would have 11% higher ($15 million higher revenue adjusted for 20% price decline) if the pharmacy would have been able to deliver the unfulfilled Rx orders for a pro forma growth rate closer to +60% (+49% plus +11%).

Next Year the Negative Effects of Unfulfilled Scripts and 20% Generic Discounts Should Reverse

The above two pro forma exercises are important because next year the negative effects of unfulfilled scripts and 20% generic price reductions taken in 2025 should reverse and add to both revenue and physical volume growth during 2026 when pharmacies catch up with demand growth and we estimate average selling prices go back up as the new better performing Relacorilant drug replaces the old Korlym drug.

*We are NOT saying that Corcept really should have delivered +52.9% revenue growth in Q2 2025 vs. Q2 2024 because they did miss on deliveries and they did introduce generic discounts. However, we are pointing out that real physical demand grew closer to +60% in Q2 versus last year which is a healthy growth rate that suggests significant early interest by the medical community. In addition, by next year as Corcept anniversaries the Authorized Generic price discounts currently underway and as it transitions in 2026 to what management suggested on the conference call would be virtually a total switching of its patient base from the older genericized Korlym drug to what we expect will be a newly FDA-approved Relacorilant drug with a far superior safety profile, the average selling price could return back up to closer to the pre-discounted listed price for Korlym.

If Relacorilant is introduced next year close to the original Korlym price levels as we believe, that would equate to about a 25% increase in average selling prices vs. 2025 average selling price levels which could result in significantly faster revenue growth in 2026 as Relacorilant occupies an increasing share of its patient base and premium prices for a premium product would be layered on top of physical volume growth which we estimate conservatively could be in the +40-50% range.

We've Made Minor Reductions to Our Revenue and Earnings Model Leaving Room for Potential Upside

Our Earnings model below produced by my partner Benjamin Bratt reflects this year's pharmacy shortfalls and minor increases in SG&A expense for an expanding salesforce and for the anticipated launches of Relacorilant for hypercortisolism and Platinum Resistant Ovarian Cancer, both of which we estimate will be approved and launching next year. However, our EPS estimate is being raised for 2028 from our April 2025 estimate of $10.10/share to $10.31/share on our optimism that the medical community is getting the message of underdiagnosis of hypercortisolism with difficult to treat Type 2 diabetics and recent increases in the salesforce.

We caution that there are a lot of moving parts and it is difficult to be exact when growth is in the accelerating part of an S-curve with potential for upside as mentioned below.

Upside

Despite the second quarter's encouraging early signs of penetration of the nascent difficult to treat Type 2 diabetes market suggested by the 49% Q2 volume growth, we are choosing to be conservative with our model estimates, waiting to see progress on:

  1. Capacity additions by the specialty pharmacy and the second pharmacy in Q4.
  2. Sales results from the recent tripling of the salesforce.
  3. Approval of Relacorilant for hypercortisolism by the 12/30/25 PDUFA date and pace of the subsequent launch and switching of patients from Korlym.
  4. Approval for Relacorilant for Platinum Resistant Ovarian Cancer which could come as early as September 2026 if given Priority Review and receive a March 2026 PDUFA date plus 6 months -- or as late as January 2027 if given a standard FDA review and a July 2026 PDUFA date plus 6 months.
  5. Potential of fast track approval for Dazucorilant for ALS given its 84% reduction in death in the first year announced June 5, 2025 for Corcept's Phase 2 trial.

Valuation

Note that at $67.17/share Corcept is currently valued at $8.09 billion based on 120.5 million shares fully diluted, or only 2.7 to 1.6 times projected $3 billion to $5 billion of annual revenue for just Relacorilant for hypercortisolism alone.

On a P/E basis on our EPS estimates, Corcept is being valued at 58.9 x $1.27 for 2025, 19.1 x $3.52 for 2026, 9.6 x $7.01 for 2027 and 6.5 x $10.31 for 2028.

Our target price for Corcept in 2 years (2027) is for CORT to be valued at the same conservative P/E that we used in our last April 15, 2025 report of 17.7 times the next year's earnings (2028). This would produce a target price in 2027 of 17.7 x $10.31/share = $182/share, or slightly higher than our prior target of $178/share when we had a 2028 EPS estimate of $10.10/share.

Risks

In my decades of experience as an analyst and institutional investor, I have seen that managing growth rates faster than 25-30% per year is very difficult to maintain, and Corcept is certainly experiencing growing pains. We would not be surprised if there are additional hiccups along the way but point out that as the company grows its patient base and diversifies by entering large new markets for cortisol modulation including oncology, neurology, and other therapeutic areas, we believe that Corcept will be able to generate more steady but still rapid double digit growth for years to come.

Near term, it will be critical for Corcept's specialty pharmacies to execute for the hypercortisolism franchise to capitalize on its current early momentum, and we will have answers soon.

Given the risky nature of drug development, there are likely to be disappointing results from clinical trials. However, we believe that the Corcept's cortisol modulating science platform is based on a solid foundation of industry-leading experience and research and that many of their 1000 identified cortisol modulators will emerge to expand the hypercortisolism franchise and find large new therapeutic markets, and diversify Corcept into a leading broad-based Platform Growth Company.

Long-Term Outlook Remains Robust

In our opinion the long-term outlook remains quite promising for multiple years if not decades of strong double digit growth for Corcept, and the long term appears to be closer to reality than we have been anticipating if indeed the medical community is accepting that hypercortisolism is significantly underdiagnosed and on a faster ramp than we expected for testing difficult to treat Type 2 diabetics.

On a prior conference call management predicted that its Cushings hypercortisolism franchise could generate $3 to 5 billion in annual revenue in 3 to 5 years. On today's call they reiterated that prediction and explained that they "expect almost all patients to transition to Relacorilant, that growth will accelerate when it becomes available and that in the next 3 to 5 years Relacorilant will generate $3 to 5 billion of (annual) revenue in hypercortisolism alone...and we don't think the $3 to 5 billion is peak sales."

This article was written by Graham Tanaka, CFA (Tanaka Capital Management), President and CIO of Tanaka Capital Management (website: tanaka.com) and manager of the Tanaka Growth Fund (TGFRX). Brown University Ab.ScB Civil Engineering/Urban Studies, Stanford University MBA, Morgan Guaranty Trust (JP Morgan), Fiduciary Trust International, Tanaka Capital Mgmt., Author "Digital Deflation" McGraw-Hill 2004. Appeared on CNBC and Bloomberg TV 40+ times. Chartered Financial Analyst (CFA). Registered Investment Advisor (RIA), Director, Council For Economic Education (CEE). Manage portfolios for pension funds, foundations, families, individuals and a public no-load mutual fund. Investment expertise in Technology, Healthcare, Financials, Consumer, Metals & Mining, Energy & Industrials with focus on finding Platform GrowthTM Companies early.

data.title: Baron Capital's Top Health Care Stock Picks And Misses From Q2 2025
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from Baron Health Care Fund Q2 2025 Shareholder Letter.

During the quarter, we added seven new positions and exited seven positions. Below we discuss some of our top net purchases and sales.

Top net purchases for the quarter

Quarter End Market Cap ($B) Net Amount Purchased ($M)
Masimo (MASI) Corporation 9.1 3.0
Exact Sciences Corporation (EXAS) 10.0 2.5
Eli Lilly (LLY) and Company 738.8 2.1
Penumbra (PEN), Inc. 9.9 2.1
Edwards Lifesciences Corporation (EW) 45.9 1.9

We added to our position in Masimo Corporation, a medical device company that manufactures non-invasive patient monitoring technologies, such as pulse oximetry for measuring blood oxygen levels of patients in the hospital. The company finalized the divestiture of its consumer audio business, which had been a distraction from their core health care business. The company plans to prioritize share repurchases with the proceeds from the sale. The trade war and tariff uncertainty has been an overhang, given Masimo manufactures products in Mexico, Malaysia, and China, but there is reason to be optimistic about de-escalation and trade deals. After meeting with new CEO Katie Szyman, we feel confident in her vision which is focused on achieving commercial excellence, bringing Masimo’s AI technology to the hospital, and expanding the company's overall patient monitoring footprint within the hospital. We think the core health care business can continue to grow in the high single digits on the top line, and cost rationalization and disciplined spending should enable the company to more than double EPS within five years.

We initiated a position in Exact Sciences Corporation, a molecular diagnostics company focused on the early detection of colorectal cancer. The company is best known for its non-invasive colorectal cancer stool tests, Cologuard and Cologuard Plus. 106 million adults in the U.S. are eligible for colorectal cancer screening, of which half are not up to date. Colonoscopies are the gold standard for colorectal cancer screening, but they are invasive and require significant preparation. In contrast, Cologuard is non-invasive and requires significantly less time commitment. Cologuard adoption is growing and the test now accounts for around 13% of colorectal cancer screening. We are increasingly hearing from doctors and patients that they are considering Cologuard ahead of colonoscopies. Shares underperformed in 2024 as adoption of Cologuard slowed, as investors anticipate competition from Guardant (GH) Health which launched a colorectal cancer blood test, and as investors feared a worst-case outcome from a Supreme Court case that potentially has implications for insurance coverage mandates for Cologuard. We think shares are well positioned to re-rate as each fear is dispelled: (1) we expect Exact Science's commercial restructuring to continue to drive volume growth reacceleration; (2) Guardant's blood test is less sensitive than Cologuard and we think blood tests will be relegated to patients who refuse more sensitive screening options; and (3) at the end of June, the Supreme Court issued its decision in the Braidwood case which preserved the insurance coverage mandate. We think Cologuard is a compelling option for colorectal cancer screening and Exact Sciences will continue to penetrate this market.

We added to our position in Eli Lilly and Company, a global pharmaceutical company best known for developing and selling GLP-1 medications for diabetes and obesity. The most recent generation of GLP-1 drugs (brand names Mounjaro and Zepbound) not only offer superb blood sugar control for diabetics, but also can drive 20% or greater weight loss and likely improve cardiovascular outcomes in both diabetic and non-diabetic obese patients. We think these drugs will become the standard of care for both diabetes and obesity, and will become a $150 billion category. We see Lilly as the leader in the space, setting a high efficacy bar with their GLP-1s on the market and continuing to innovate and develop next-generation medications that are more effective and more convenient. We are excited about once-daily oral orforglipron, which recently showed Phase 3 data in type 2 diabetes that rivaled Novo Nordisk (NVO)'s injectable Ozempic. We expect Phase 3 orforglipron in obesity this summer to also be competitive with Novo Nordisk's injectable Wegovy. Beyond orforglipron, Lilly is also studying a high efficacy injectable (retatrutide) in Phase 3, an amylin hormone analogue (eloralintide) that can be combined with Zepbound in Phase 2, and a muscle-preserving drug bimagrumab (which can also be combined with Zepbound) in Phase 2. We continue to think this market is in the early innings of uptake, we think the adoption of GLP-1s will drive Lilly to triple its total revenues by 2030, and we think Lilly has the pipeline portfolio of drugs to sustain its leadership position.

We added to our position in Penumbra, Inc., a medical device company that manufactures mechanical thrombectomy devices to remove clots. We think Penumbra’s products are differentiated because of their computer assisted vacuum thrombectomy (CAVT) technology. CAVT utilizes algorithms to modulate the aspiration power depending on if a clot is detected, and to control a separate valve that injects saline to reduce friction between the clot and catheter. This maximizes clot removal while decreasing risk of blood loss. For U.S. thrombectomy, we see a market opportunity for over 1 million procedures a year across stroke and peripheral vascular, with a total addressable market (TAM) north of $8 billion. Of this TAM, Penumbra is only high single digits percentage penetrated in 2024. We are looking forward to this mechanism rolling out in the stroke setting (the Thunderbolt product which is in development). In addition, the company is seeing strong momentum in the venous setting already with their Flash product. We think this momentum could accelerate if the Storm-PE trial, which completed enrollment during the quarter, demonstrates superiority of Penumbra’s CAVT plus anticoagulation versus anticoagulation alone (the current standard of care) in intermediate-high risk pulmonary embolism patients. The trial could change the treatment paradigm for pulmonary embolism.

We reacquired shares of Edwards Lifesciences Corporation, a leading manufacturer of heart valve replacement and repair products. Transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) is a minimally invasive procedure to treat aortic stenosis, a disease that obstructs the flow of blood out of the aortic valve, which strains the heart. Edwards Lifesciences has a leading position in the TAVR market backed by a robust body of clinical evidence and physician familiarity with the product and workflow. TAVR is a $5 billion market that still continues to grow many years after Edwards Lifesciences first entered the market. We expect this growth to continue at a moderate pace as indications expand to cover a broader patient population. In addition, competitor Boston Scientific (BSX) Corporation recently exited the TAVR market, which provides incremental market share opportunity for Edwards Lifesciences.

Besides Edwards Lifesciences’ leading position in TAVR, the company also offers products for the mitral and tricuspid valves. These are other valves in the heart that, if dysfunctional, can also cause the heart to work harder and become strained. We believe that transcatheter tricuspid valve replacement represents the most exciting growth driver for Edwards Lifesciences because tricuspid regurgitation is an area with high unmet need and has the potential to be just as large as the TAVR TAM, if not larger. We believe Edwards Lifesciences is uniquely positioned here with their tricuspid valve replacement product Evoque, which is in the early stages of the commercial launch. We believe over the long term, Edwards Lifesciences can grow revenue in the high single digits to low double digits and EPS mid-teens.

Top net sales for the quarter

Net Amount Sold ($M)
UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) 12.5
Boston Scientific Corporation 6.0
Intuitive Surgical (ISRG), Inc. 5.0
Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) Inc. 4.2
Arcellx, Inc. (ACLX) 1.9

We sold UnitedHealth Group Incorporated because the company lowered guidance shortly after reporting earnings and then suspended guidance shortly after that. Management cited three issues: greater-than-expected impact from the health status of new members, higher-than-expected cost trends in the Medicare Advantage business, and a broadening of this higher trend to the Medicaid and commercial business. Some of these issues could potentially be fixed in the short term through repricing, but we felt management’s lack of visibility raised too many questions about the long-term earnings potential of the business. We plan to revisit the investment thesis after new management conducts its comprehensive review and resets earnings guidance.

We reduced Boston Scientific Corporation and Intuitive Surgical, Inc. to manage risk after the position sizes increased from multi-year share appreciation. We maintain a positive view about the long-term outlook for both companies.

We reduced Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. because several headwinds are impacting the business, including NIH funding cuts, potential tariffs on the company and its biopharmaceutical customers, a biotechnology funding slowdown, and slower growth in China.

We reduced Arcellx, Inc. to manage portfolio risk.

data.title: Baron Health Care Fund Q2: Top Contributors, Misses, And Strategic Shifts
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from Baron Health Care Fund Q2 2025 Shareholder Letter.

Top Contributors & Detractors

Top contributors to performance for the quarter

Contribution to Return (%)
Insmed (INSM) Incorporated 0.55
Intuitive Surgical (ISRG), Inc. 0.42
IDEXX Laboratories, Inc. (IDXX) 0.33
HealthEquity, Inc. (HQY) 0.30
RadNet, Inc. (RDNT) 0.27

Insmed Incorporated is a biotechnology company with three lead pulmonology drugs that collectively have the potential to generate more than $8 billion in peak sales. We are particularly excited about TPIP, a once-daily prostanoid in development for PAH and PH associated with interstitial lung disease. Prostacyclin therapies are a mainstay for these conditions and currently generate about $4.5 billion in global sales. We believe TPIP could become the most effective option in the class, with significantly more convenient dosing. Shares rose after Phase 2 data for TPIP in PAH exceeded investor expectations. We are also positive on brensocatib for non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis, which is a $5 billion-plus opportunity. Physician feedback and survey data suggests the drug could be transformative, showing a 20% reduction in pulmonary exacerbations and improved lung function. We believe the disease is widely underdiagnosed (often misdiagnosed as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease) and could affect up to 500,000 patients in the U.S.

Intuitive Surgical, Inc. manufactures the da Vinci Surgical System, a robotic surgical system used for minimally invasive surgical procedures. The stock contributed to performance on solid financial results. Intuitive continues to generate strong procedure growth and is in the early stages of a new product cycle with its da Vinci 5 system. We continue to view Intuitive as a competitively advantaged business with durable moats, including proprietary technology, a strong portfolio of patents, regulatory approvals, a large installed base of robotic systems, a growing base of customers trained on its platform, and a robust balance sheet. We believe the company has a long runway for growth driven by the continued adoption and expansion of robotic surgery.

Shares of veterinary diagnostics leader IDEXX Laboratories, Inc. contributed to performance for the quarter after reporting better-than-expected financial results. Foot traffic to veterinary clinics in the U.S. remains under pressure, which has continued to hamper aggregate revenue growth. Despite macroeconomic challenges, IDEXX’s excellent execution has enabled the company to maintain strong performance. We believe competitive trends are outstanding, and we expect new proprietary innovations and field sales force expansion to be meaningful contributors to growth in 2025. We see increasing evidence that long-term secular trends around pet ownership and pet care spending have structurally accelerated, which should help support IDEXX’s long-term growth rate.

Top detractors from performance for the quarter

Contribution to Return (%)
UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) (3.72)
Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO) (0.69)
argenx SE (ARGX)(OTCPK:ARGNF) (0.67)
The Cooper Companies (COO), Inc. (0.45)
Elevance Health, Inc. (ELV) (0.41)

UnitedHealth Group Incorporated is a diversified health and well-being company with $450 billion in annual revenue, operating across four segments: UnitedHealthcare, Optum Health, Optum lnsight, and Optum Rx. Shares fell sharply during the quarter after the company missed earnings estimates and cut its 2025 earnings per share guidance, citing higher-than-expected medical costs in its Medicare Advantage business. Investor confidence was further shaken in early May by the abrupt departure of CEO Andrew Witty and the suspension of 2025 guidance. The company also mispriced its Medicare Advantage business for 2025—a challenge compounded by reimbursement changes and an influx of newly acquired members who had not been properly risk coded. While we acknowledge UnitedHealth’s potential to restore profitability in this segment over time through disciplined pricing and benefit adjustments, we chose to exit our position during the quarter in favor of other opportunities.

Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. is a life sciences company that offers instruments and consumables for research, tools for bioproduction, specialty diagnostics, and contract research and manufacturing services. Shares detracted from performance during the quarter. The life sciences segment remains under pressure due to continued grant cancellations by the National Institutes of Health (NIH) and persistent funding constraints at universities. We retain conviction as Thermo Fisher is dominant across multiple end markets and its scale gives it resilience. Once the macroeconomic environment stabilizes, we expect the company to resume organic growth in the high single-digit range and deliver double-digit earnings-per-share growth.

Argenx SE is a biotechnology company best known for developing Vyvgart, the leading FcRn inhibitor for the treatment of autoimmune conditions. Shares declined after Q1 Vyvgart sales came in below elevated investor expectations due to a combination of seasonal factors (such as insurance reverification) and higher Medicare Part D utilization and associated discounts. Our conversations with management and neurologists continue to reinforce Vyvgart’s value as an important treatment option with strong long-term growth potential. The drug continues to launch well in both generalized myasthenia gravis and chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy. Over time, we expect Vyvgart to demonstrate efficacy across an expanding range of autoantibody-driven autoimmune conditions, supported by encouraging Phase 2 myositis data recently presented by argenx at a major medical conference.

data.title: 1-800-Flowers: Shifting Consumption Patterns Exacerbate Financial Deterioration
data.description:
Introduction

Based on my analysis, although 1-800-FLOWERS.COM's (NASDAQ:FLWS) share price has plunged 80% from its high, the company is still expensive, and there are no compelling reasons to initiate a position. Currently, the company is still facing multiple headwinds, exacerbating its current revenue deterioration. Even though FLWS introduced a turnaround strategy, structural challenges such as freight disruptions and tariffs may offset any potential operational improvements. In this report, I will demonstrate why investors should avoid FLWS.

Company Overview

Founded in 1976, FLWS is a retail company that sells gift-related products such as plants, flowers, and fruit baskets in the United States and internationally. Today, FLWS is headquarted in New York and employs more than 4,000 employees across the world.

Unknown to many, FLWS offers its products and services through more than 20 platforms and brands such as florists.com, The Popcorn Factory, and Simply Chocolate. According to the company's most recent filings, FLWS operates through three segments: (1) Consumer Floral & Gifts, (2) Gourmet Food & Gift Baskets, and (3) BloomNet.

Recent Developments

For 3Q25, FLWS reported exceptionally disappointing results. The company generated $331.45 million in revenue, representing a decline of 12.6% year-on-year and 57.3% quarter-on-quarter. During this period, operating and net income margins have eroded substantially. Operating margin have deteriorated from -7.36% to -58.36% while net income margin took a nosedive from -4.46% to 53.78%.

The primary reason for the company's margin deterioration is due to a huge surge in COGS and OPEX. As a reference, FLWS's COGS and OPEX margin is currently 68.32% and 90.04%; as compared to the same period last year, COGS and OPEX margin increased by 139 bps and 4,960 bps respectively.

In the most recent earnings release, FLWS's management withdrew guidance for FY2025, indicating a lack of confidence and suggesting that FLWS will continue to experience deteriorated performance.

Global Economic Uncertainties, Changing Consumption Patterns and Competitive Pressures Will Further Accelerate Demand Destruction

By now, investors who have been following the markets closely will know that we are in the early stages of a global economic downturn. Based on the most recent data, the labor market is showing signs of severe deterioration; labor market participation rate is declining while U6 unemployment rate have surged to 7.9%. In such an environment, it is not surprising to know that consumer confidence deteriorates while credit card defaults spike to new highs.

More relevant to FLWS, consumers' consumption patterns are changing. For those who intend to control their expenditures, more than half intends to dial back on non-essential spending. According to the International Fresh Produce Association, 66% of consumers are reducing overall expenditures while 50% of consumers are intending to buy in lower volumes.

In-line with the bleak consumer environment, FLWS's retail segments for 3Q25 saw substantial revenue declines. Consumer Floral & Gifts fell by 11.39% year-on-year while Gourmet Foods & Gift Baskets plunged by 18.24%. As long as the economic uncertainty persists, it is very likely that FLWS will continue to experience demand destruction.

Apart from macro-related headwinds, FLWS is also facing intense competitive pressures that will further affect the company's topline. Current data suggests that other forms of gifting are outpacing non-floral and traditional forms of gifting (e.g., gift baskets); for example, interest for Amazon Gift Cards have increased 342% in December 2024 as compared to that of July 2024. Many major brands such as Starbucks (SBUX) and even technological giants such as Apple have also moved into the gifting industry, offering consumers more practical alternatives with higher values.

FLWS's Financial Health Suggests That Its Business Model Is At Risk

In today's public markets, most publicly listed multi-decade companies have established themselves as stable cashflow generators despite low revenue growth. However, FLWS not only saw its FCF declining but also recorded staggering cumulative losses.

For the past two years, FLWS recorded a cumulative net loss of $165.19 million. From a quarterly perspective, FLWS recorded losses in 7 out of the past 10 quarters. Naturally, the company's balance sheet has also deteriorated. Debt to Equity has increased to 0.44 from 0.34 in 2022 while Days Payables rose to 26.63 from 15.11 in 2022, suggesting that the company is attempting to preserve liquidity.

Although FLWS's has recently announced its new long-term strategy to recover revenue and reduce costs, Celebrations Wave, it is still in the nascent stages. Given the company's poor decision making track record (e.g. shutting down retail stores) in the past few years, it is reasonable for investors to remain skeptical and wait for actual results that prove that FLWS can improve its operations.

Moreover, some of these challenges such as freight disruptions and tariffs are highly structural; there is only so much that FLWS can do on the operations level, suggesting that the company's business model is at risk. Based on the latest earnings call, FLWS's management have revealed that about $70 million (i.e., around 6.7%) of the company's COGS is imported and subjected to tariffs. Given that the company is already unprofitable, the surge in COGS due to tariffs will further weigh on FLWS's turnaround efforts.

Valuation Analysis Suggests FLWS's Share Price Is Overvalued By 98%

To ascertain the share price of FLWS, I have conducted a DCF-based valuation analysis based on the following assumptions and reasons:

  • Revenue to grow by 3.8% per annum for the next five years. This assumption is chosen to reflect the possibility that FLWS is able to withstand the current headwinds and recover its revenue to FY2022 levels in the next five years.
  • OPEX margin to improve by 1% per annum for the next five years. This assumption is chosen to reflect the possibility that FLWS is able to reduce expenses (e.g., customer acquisition costs) as part of its Celebrations Wave strategy.
  • Terminal growth of 2% and WACC of 9.79%. WACC is based on 33% debt and 67% equity. As a reference, the company's estimated cost of debt and cost of equity of 5.17% and 12.04%. I have specifically chosen a lower cost of debt to price in a more optimistic scenario for FLWS.

Based on these assumptions, which can be considered fairly optimistic, my model suggests that the share price of FLWS should be $2.83. Currently, FLWS trades at a price of $5.62, suggesting that the company is 98% overvalued. It is important to highlight that despite factoring optimistic assumptions, FLWS is still considered overvalued.

Closing Remarks

Overall, even though FLWS share price has fell close to 80% from its high, the company is still extremely overvalued. Moreover, the company is facing multiple headwinds ranging such as shifting consumption patterns and increasing competitive pressures; there is a strong likelihood that its revenues will continue to decline in the foreseeable future.

It is important to note that other structural risks such as tariffs and freight disruptions will also continue to eat into the company's margins, challenging the company's ability to turnaround its operations. Therefore, I believe that investors should avoid FLWS entirely; there are better places to put your money to work.

Finally, FLWS is set to report earnings on 4th September 2025. Investors should track whether the company's Celebrations Wave strategy has improved demand. Also, the company had previously stated that they may consider other solutions such as passing on costs to consumers and re-examining their suppliers to offset higher tariffs. Therefore, investors should monitor whether FLWS has successfully implemented these improvements to offset the potential higher costs coming from tariffs.
